the popular pipeline of existing CL methods is to directly load the union of new class data and a few exemplars
The popular pipeline of existing CL methods involves directly loading the union of new class data Dt and a few exemplars M1:t−1 to train the model f (·; θ).
Due to memory constraint, the performance of the pipeline is typically dependent on selecting representative exemplars.
Naive uniting a large amount of new data and a few exemplars causes high bias to recently learned tasks and quick forgetting of old tasks.
Issues like high bias and quick forgetting can be alleviated by techniques like exemplar sampling strategies.
Techniques like increasing the model capacity for learning new tasks can also help in mitigating the limitations.
A desirable continual learning system should scale to a large number of tasks without excessive human prior and increasing parameter overheads.
Bilateral Memory Consolidation (BiMeCo) is developed to mitigate the limitations of existing CL methods.
BiMeCo explicitly decouples the memory mechanism into short-term phase and long-term phase for consolidating the structured knowledge.
Mining structured knowledge from memory samples is key in consolidating the knowledge across all learned tasks.
The short-term phase focuses on expressiveness with a large amount of training samples and rapid adaptation to recent tasks to form working memory.

The long-term phase learns a few task-balance samples online to consolidate working memory for guiding the short-term phase.
The long-term phase consolidates working memory to prevent forgetting in the short-term phase.
The long-term phase inherits strong expressiveness from the short-term phase.
